summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – INTERIM SPOUSAL MAINTENANCE – Where the husband contends that the wife cannot establish that she is unable to support herself and in the alternative that he does not have capacity to meet the orders sought by the wife – Where consideration of s 75(2) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) factors supports the reasonableness of the wife’s conduct and the quantifications of her needs – Where the husband’s priority as to the wife’s role in being available to care for the primary school aged children of the parties has shifted after separation – Where the husband has sources of financial resources available to him to fund the wife’s claim for maintenance – Orders for interim periodic and non-periodic spousal maintenance made as sought by the wife.

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAMPTON J:

These reasons determine the interim relief prosecuted by Ms Quinones (“the wife”) in her Initiating Application filed on 23 February 2026 seeking orders for periodic and non-periodic spousal maintenance payable by Mr Quinones (“the husband”). The husband in a Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 19 March 2026 seeks that the wife’s relief be dismissed.

BACKGROUND

The parties commenced cohabitation in 2010 and married in 2012. They separated on a final basis on 16 September 2023. An Order for Divorce was made in 2024.
There are two primary school aged children of the marriage, X born in 2015 and Y born in 2016 (collectively referred to as “the children”).
The husband filed an Initiating Application in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) on 22 March 2024 seeking orders as to the parenting of the children pursuant to Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). The wife filed a Response to an Initiating Application on 9 May 2024 seeking different orders regulating the parenting of the children. She filed an Amended Response to an Initiating Application on 20 December 2024 seeking further orders as to the adjustment of the property of the parties pursuant to s 79 of the Act.
The husband’s parents, Mr B Quinones and Mrs B Quinones were joined to the proceedings as the second and third respondents on 18 March 2025. The fourth respondent, C Pty Ltd, in its capacity as the corporate trustee of the Quinones Family Trust, being a discretionary trust controlled by the husband’s parents, was joined as a party to the proceedings on 24 June 2025. The husband’s parents and the fourth respondent are plaintiffs in the proceedings for relief seeking to recover loans secured upon the real property of the husband and the wife, or in the alternative seeking equitable relief by way of the imposition of a resulting trust upon their legal interests in the real property. The wife challenges the fact and terms of aspects of the contended loans and opposes the equitable relief. The husband submits to the relief claimed by his parents. The financial proceedings have some complexity.
The parenting of the children is regulated by interim orders made on a defended basis by a judge in Division 2 on 2 August 2024, broadly providing that the children primarily live with the wife for 10 nights each fortnight during the school-term and spend half of the school holidays with the father. It seems uncontroversial that the children, in addition to school, undertake extracurricular activities including dancing and soccer outside of school hours.
The financial and parenting proceedings were transferred to Division 1 on 27 June 2025.
The wife by way of an Initiating Application filed in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) on 23 February 2026 commenced proceedings as to spousal maintenance. Those proceedings were transferred to Division 1 on 13 March 2026 and consolidated with the financial and parenting proceedings.
The husband consents to an order pursuant to s 44(3) of the Act extending the time for the wife to file her application for her own maintenance until 23 February 2026. The husband’s parents and C Pty Ltd as trustee for the Quinones Family Trust did not participate in the hearing of the interim spousal maintenance application today.

DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON:

The wife relied on:
(a) Her Initiating Application filed on 23 February 2026;

(b) Her affidavit filed on 4 March 2026; and

(c) Her Financial Statement filed on 23 February 2026.

The husband relied on:
(a) His Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 19 March 2026;

(b) His affidavit filed on 19 March 2026;

(c) His Financial Statement filed on 19 March 2026;

(d) The wife’s Financial Questionnaire filed on 20 December 2024;

(e) The wife’s Financial Statement filed on 20 December 2024; and

(f) A balance sheet filed on 1 December 2025.

Each party tendered a quantity of documents in support of their relief sought.

The relief sought by the wife

The wife seeks the following interlocutory orders as contained in her Initiating Application filed on 23 February 2026:
...

Interim Spousal Maintenance
Pursuant to sections 72, 74 and 77A of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) and pending further order the Husband shall pay by way of non-periodic spousal maintenance:
(a) direct to providers the utility and rates in full as and when they fall due in relation to the [D Street], [Suburb E] Property being:
i. Electricity

ii. Gas

iii. Water

iv. Council

v. Phone and Internet
(b) the sum of $332.00 per week directly to the Wife’s Commonwealth Bank credit card ending in [#77], or to another credit card facility in the Wife’s name nominated by the Wife in writing by way of BPAY.
The first payment shall be made within two (2) days of the date of these Orders and thereafter fortnightly, on the same weekday as the first payment.

(c) the Wife’s [G Company] Private health insurance policy ([...]) direct to the provider, as and when it falls due.

(d) The costs associated with motor vehicle driven by the Wife (Registration [...]), direct to the provider, as and when they fall due including.

i. Insurance

ii. compulsory third party (CTP) insurance; and

iii. Registration
(e) the [F Company] Home insurance policy ([...]) of the former Marital home where the Children and Wife reside, direct to the provider, as and when it falls due.
Interim Provision of Private Health Insurance Information
pending further order and in accordance with Order 2 (c), the Wife shall provide to the Husband within fourteen (14) days of these Orders:
(a) the [G Limited] Private health insurance policy ([...]) including all payment details; and

(b) a supplementary card, or equivalent access arrangements, enabling the Husband utilise the policy for the benefit of the children during the time they are in his care.

the Husband shall provide to the Wife written confirmation of each payment made pursuant to Order 2(c) within seven (7) days of payment.

...

THE LAW

The High Court in Hall & Hall [2016] HCA 23; (2016) 257 CLR 490 (“Hall”) set out the appropriate approach in considering an application for interim spousal maintenance (at 496):
...The gateway to the operation of Pt VIII in relation to spousal maintenance is in s 72(1). That sub-section provides that “[a] party to a marriage is liable to maintain the other party, to the extent that the first-mentioned party is reasonably able to do so, if, and only if, that other party is unable to support herself or himself adequately ... having regard to any relevant matter referred to in [s] 75(2)”.

The liability of a party to a marriage to maintain the other party that is imposed by s 72(1) is crystallised by the making of an order under s 74(1). That sub-section provides that, “[i]n proceedings with respect to the maintenance of a party to a marriage, the court may make such order as it considers proper for the provision of maintenance in accordance with this Part”.

A court exercising the power conferred by s 74(1) is obliged by s 75(1) to take into account the matters referred to in s 75(2) and only those matters. Those matters are presented as a comprehensive checklist. They include what s 75(2)(b) refers to as “the income, property and financial resources of each of the parties and the physical and mental capacity of each of them for appropriate gainful employment”. They also include, by virtue of s 75(2)(o), “any fact or circumstance which, in the opinion of the court, the justice of the case requires to be taken into account”.

(Footnotes omitted)
The four step process as to whether an order for spousal maintenance should be made are:
(a) To what extent is the wife unable to support herself?

(b) What are the wife’s reasonable needs?

(c) What capacity does the husband have to meet an order?

(d) If steps (a) to (c) favour the wife what order is reasonable having regard to s 75(2)?

The husband submits that the wife has not achieved the threshold mandated by steps (a) and (b). If accepted, the enquiry need progress no further, and the wife’s application will be dismissed.
The threshold finding is whether the wife is “unable to support herself adequately”, not only by reason of one of the matters set out in (a), (b) and (c) of s 72, but also having regard to any relevant matters as set out in s 75(2) of the Act.
“Adequately” is not determined by reference to any fixed or absolute standard but by reference to the matters referred to in s 75(2). The reasonableness of the wife’s conduct in all the circumstances is the key to the exercise of any discretion in her favour – this includes the quantification of her needs and an assessment as to her own efforts in making provision for her own self-support.

CONSIDERATION

I accept the evidence of the wife that she has not been employed outside the family home since 2012 except for some limited casual work from that time until 2015. She was engaged in what she described as a “registered art business” trading as H Company from mid-2021.
It is uncontroversial that a dynamic existed between the parties during their marriage relationship that the wife would be available to care for the children before and after school, to assist them in attending their after-school activities and to otherwise be available for them. As between she and the husband, during the period of the marriage she had the primary day-to-day care of the children. As part of that marriage dynamic, the husband was the primary income earner.
The husband asserts the wife has some capacity to earn an income as an “artist”. He set out in his affidavit the gross income received by the wife from sales as an artist from 2021 to 2023. His evidence is that the wife’s gross income from sales of artworks was $19,657 in 2023, $4,776 in 2024, and $2,500 in 2025. This construction of the husband’s evidence does him little credit. An analysis of the wife’s taxation returns records, amongst other things, that in the year ending 30 June 2023 her net profit was $1,591, for the year ending 30 June 2024 her net loss was $3,867, and for the year ending 30 June 2025 her net loss was $8,636.
The husband further asserts by way of a bundle of documents tendered, originating from Indeed, Seek, or other similar websites, that the wife has some unexercised capacity to obtain and engage in employment during school hours. During exchanges in submissions at least the first five of those documents require a potential employee to have skills and/or availabilities outside those currently existing with the wife.
The husband identifies that the wife, by way of a letter to his solicitors dated 23 March 2026, said that she has received no commissions for any artwork in the past 12 months and she has made no job applications. Against that background he broadly asserts that the wife has chosen not to seek employment and implicitly not exercise her income earning capacity.
The evidence adduced as to the wife’s capacity to gain employment on consideration of the dynamic that these parties employed over the period of their marriage leads to the conclusion, on an interlocutory basis, that the wife has little capacity at the present time to obtain permanent employment.
The evidence as to the wife’s needs are contained in Part G and Part N of her Financial Statement filed on 23 February 2026. It is the wife’s evidence that her credit card has been applied to meet periodic expenses and to include them as an ongoing expense would be a double counting. The total cost of her periodic needs as set out therein is asserted to be $686, being:
Part G - Personal Expenditure	Wife’s average weekly amount
Rates, unit levies	$69
Private health insurance	$56
Home insurance	$31
Car insurance	$37
Total	$193
Part N – Orders for maintenance, child support, financial enforcement	Wife’s average weekly amount
Food	$205
Household supplies	$10
Gas	$6
Electricity	$23
Internet	$8
Telephone	$11
Petrol	$30
Maintenance	$10
Fares/car parking	$16
Clothing and shoes	$60
Medical, dental and optical (not including health insurance

premiums)	$17
Entertainment/hobbies	$20
Chemist/pharmaceutical	$10
Repairs - furnishings and appliances	$5
Dry cleaning	$5
Books and magazines, other

subscriptions or streaming services	$11
Gifts	$20
Hairdressing, toiletries, personal care	$26
Total	$493
The wife drives Motor Vehicle 1 owned by J Pty Ltd. Each of the husband and the wife are the shareholders of that corporation. The husband is the sole director. The husband appears to contend that the corporation currently does not trade.
The wife has savings of $432, some furniture, jewellery and watches. The wife and the children occupy the property at D Street, Suburb E to which the husband’s parents and the fourth respondent in its capacity as trustee for the discretionary trust lay claim. The wife is in arrears of:
(a) $2,346.12 for electricity;

(b) $339.11 for gas;

(c) $678.13 for water; and

(d) $2,218.17 for council rates.

The wife’s Financial Statement filed on 20 December 2024 recorded that she had borrowed $180,000 up to that time for legal fees from her mother. Her current debt to her mother is $200,000, being the value of her mother’s loan facility. It is the wife’s evidence that she has met her periodic shortfall of expenses in excess of her income and otherwise funded that shortfall by way of her credit card debt currently slightly under $3,000 and the balance of monies obtained by way of loan from her mother.
While the husband in his affidavit made criticism as to some of the wife’s expenses such as food, car washing, electricity, childminding, entertainment gifts and other expenses as being implicitly inflated or unreasonable, his own Financial Statement filed on 19 March 2026 asserts not dissimilar personal expenses. He says that his rent was paid by his parents until early this year and that he now pays his rent.
On consideration of all of the evidence I find the wife has achieved the s 72 threshold having regard to her role in parenting the children during the marriage, which does not suddenly alter on separation, by way of her current care of the children and their arrangements, and by way of the fact of her being unable to meet her reasonable periodic costs of self-support from her income, property and resources. I find that the wife’s reasonable weekly costs of self-support are in the range of $686 per week. Other than receiving child support from the husband, she is in receipt of income tested Commonwealth benefits.
A child support assessment was issued in January 2024. There has been a plethora of applications of each of the parties to change or vary the administrative assessments of child support including applications for review, determinations of objections, and an Administrative Review Tribunal determination. In July 2024, the child support registrar determined the husband’s income for child support purposes was $120,000 per annum. His objection to this determination in October 2025 was unsuccessful. He appealed from that determination to the Administrative Review Tribunal in May 2025. In February 2026, the Administrative Review Tribunal determined that his income for child support purposes was $67,000 from 1 January 2026. He is currently assessed to pay child support in the sum of $162.48 per week. Payments are in arrears of approximately $1,600.
The wife asserts that the corporate trustee of the husband’s parent’s discretionary trust, the fourth respondent in the substantive proceedings, provided him through J Pty Ltd, subcontracting work throughout the period of the marriage and that the husband received not insubstantial income from that work. The husband says that from October 2023 his parents’ trust scaled down its business operations. He continues to be able to use Motor Vehicle 2 owned by the fourth respondent corporate trustee on occasions.
The husband says he is now employed as a tradesperson on a casual basis for LLPty Ltd. The wife says LL Pty Ltd does work in Region L. The husband says he receives a wage of about $34 per hour or $800 per week. This would be in the range of about $40,000 per annum. He says $100 per week is being garnisheed by the child support agency to pay the child support arrears.
The husband’s not insignificant excess of his periodic expenses over his income is striking. His rent of $832 per week is more than his income. He has been able to meet his periodic expenses, recorded in his Financial Statement as being $634 without rent or child support, notwithstanding his reduced income after 2023 by way of loan funds advanced from his parents.
The husband’s parents have also advanced the husband funds in relation to his legal fees. The documents in the husband’s case record that the value of these advances are in the range of $330,000 to date.
The husband’s Costs Notice (Exhibit 2) records that he has paid his current lawyers in excess of $344,000 by way of legal fees in relation to the current proceedings, and that his costs for a subpoena hearing on Friday 20 March 2026 and today are in the range of $21,550. He anticipates spending in the range of a further $173,500 to complete the final substantive hearing.
It is uncontroversial that during the period of the marriage, each of the husband and the wife received distributions from the Quinones Family Trust.
I am satisfied on the evidence that the husband currently has two financial resources available to him to call upon to fund the wife’s maintenance as identified by the High Court in Hall (at 506–507), where the Court said:
The reference to “financial resources” in the context of s 75(2)(b) has long been correctly interpreted by the Family Court to refer to “a source of financial support which a party can reasonably expect will be available to him or her to supply a financial need or deficiency”. The requirement that the financial resource be that “of” a party no doubt implies that the source of financial support be one on which the party is capable of drawing. It must involve something more than an expectation of benevolence on the part of another. But it goes too far to suggest that the party must control the source of financial support.

(Footnotes omitted)
The first financial resource is his income earning capacity. Historically, he had a capacity to work on a full-time basis. There is no evidence that he cannot continue to work on a full-time basis. The child support agency, by way of an enquiry, determined that his current income earning capacity for child support purposes in this current calendar year is $67,000, being in the range of $27,000 greater than his current earnings. Putting it another way, in the range of an extra $520 per week.
The second financial resource is his parents, the second and third respondents to the substantive Pt VIII litigation, who have provided him with funds to meet his reasonable living expenses in addition to meeting the costs of his legal fees. Annualised, the cost of his legal representatives for the two hearing events on last Friday and today are valued at greater than $400 per week.
By way of a not dissimilar methodology, the costs incurred by the husband in the two short listings on 20 March 2026 and today would meet the wife’s maintenance payment for eight or nine months. The value of the legal fees he will incur from now until the conclusion of the substantive trial are about five years of the value of the weekly maintenance claim by the wife.
Interlocutory spousal maintenance is directed to ensure that parties can live with decency and dignity pending determination of their substantive proceedings. The wife seeks to continue her role in being available for the children as was the case during the marriage. The husband’s priorities in relation to that role seem to have shifted after separation. The wife’s claims cannot be said in the circumstances, having regard to the husband’s financial resources, to be unreasonable. Without payment of the non-periodic payments, the wife’s costs on a weekly basis are in excess of the sum she seeks that the husband periodically contribute, those periodic costs of self-support being:
Food: $205
Household supplies: $10
Petrol: $30
Maintenance: $10
Fares/carparking: $16
Clothing and shoes: $60
Medical, dental and optical (not including health insurance): $17
Entertainment/hobbies: $20
Chemist/pharmaceutical: $10
Repairs: $5
Dry cleaning: $5
Books and magazines: $11
Gifts: $20
Hairdressing, toiletries, personal care: $26
Total: $445
I decline to make any orders as sought by the wife for the provision of G Limited private health insurance policy details to the husband. It is a matter for the parties as to whether they have two separate family policies for the children or not.
In all of the circumstances I find that the husband has the capacity to pay the non-periodic and periodic payments as sought by the wife and that the making of those orders is proper.
For all the above reasons, I make the orders set out herein.
NOTE:

These reasons have been corrected from the transcript. Topic headings have been inserted, and grammatical errors have been corrected. In addition, amendments have been made to make the orally delivered reasons clear and easy to read.
